title: Oxygen whitening powder
subtitle: A dry laundry booster with sodium percarbonate, washing soda, and citric acid for whitening and stain removal
excerpt: Sodium percarbonate releases hydrogen peroxide when it contacts water, which bleaches stains by oxidation without chlorine. Washing soda boosts the alkalinity; citric acid activates at lower temperatures than plain alkaline powder. Combined, this dry powder tackles yellowing whites and general stain removal at 40°C and above. Yield is 200 g.
sourceNotes: Sodium percarbonate (2Na2CO3.3H2O2): a dry powder that releases hydrogen peroxide and sodium carbonate on contact with water. The hydrogen peroxide bleaches by oxidation; the sodium carbonate raises pH to activate it. Effective from 30°C but best above 40°C. Does not bleach colour dyes at typical laundry temperatures. MSDS: irritant to skin, eyes, and respiratory tract; wear gloves and avoid inhaling dust. Not suitable for wool, silk, or leather. Citric acid in the blend: the acid and alkali react slowly in a dry state; to preserve the whitening power, store the powder sealed in a completely dry container and mix fresh batches every 3 months.

recipe.yieldDescription: 200 g dry powder (approximately 13 to 20 laundry doses)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale by multiplying all weights. Keep the batch size manageable; dry powders lose efficacy over time as the percarbonate slowly releases oxygen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix fresh every 3 months. Store in a sealed, airtight container away from moisture.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months in an airtight container. Sodium percarbonate slowly degrades and loses its whitening power as it releases oxygen over time, especially in humid conditions. Discard if the powder clumps or smells strongly of bleach at rest.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry powder bleaching agent that releases hydrogen peroxide and sodium carbonate when it contacts water. The hydrogen peroxide bleaches stains by oxidation rather than chlorine chemistry, making it gentler on most fabrics and colour-safe at laundry temperatures. Sold under various brand names as oxygen bleach.

content.text: : when it dissolves in wash water, it releases hydrogen peroxide, which removes stains by oxidation without the chlorine chemistry of liquid bleach. Washing soda raises the pH of the wash water, which activates the peroxide and boosts the alkaline cleaning power. A small amount of citric acid improves performance at lower wash temperatures. Together they tackle yellowing whites, sweat marks, and general organic stains. Wear protective gloves when measuring and mixing the dry powder; sodium percarbonate is an irritant to skin, eyes, and the respiratory tract. Do not use on wool, silk, or leather.

items.prepNote: The active bleaching ingredient; wear protective gloves
Sodium carbonate; raises pH and boosts cleaning power
Dry form; improves performance at lower temperatures
Mild secondary alkaliser; smooths the pH profile of the blend

content.text: Wear protective gloves and work in a ventilated space; the sodium percarbonate releases fine dust when handled. Weigh each ingredient separately. Combine all ingredients in a dry bowl and stir until evenly mixed. Do not mix with any liquid; water activates the percarbonate immediately. Store in an airtight, dry container.

For machine whitening: add 10 to 15 g directly to the drum with the laundry, not to the drawer. Wash at 40°C or above for best results. For soaking whites: dissolve 15 to 20 g in 1 litre of hot water and soak for 1 to 2 hours before rinsing and washing as normal. Not suitable for wool, silk, or leather.

Store in an airtight container in a cool, dry place away from moisture. The powder loses whitening power as the sodium percarbonate gradually releases oxygen over time; make a fresh batch every 3 months. Discard if the powder clumps significantly or smells strongly of bleach when the container is opened.
